sections.title: What quantifiers do (core logic)
sections.content:
Quantifiers are words that answer **how much?** or **how many?** before a noun. They sit in the same slot as articles (a / the) — you choose ONE determiner per noun phrase.

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: Quantifiers are determiners inside the Noun Phrase root (#3 in the Tree Model). Every time you talk about an amount — food, time, people, money — you need one. The biggest split is **countable vs uncountable**: most quantifiers prefer one or the other. Mix them up and the sentence is just wrong (e.g. *much books* is broken English, not a small mistake).

**Key principle**: First ask — is the noun countable (*book, friend, idea*) or uncountable (*water, time, advice*)? Then choose the quantifier that fits.

sections.title: some / any
sections.content:
**The default pair** — both mean an unspecified amount or number. They work with **both** countable and uncountable nouns.

- **some** — positive statements, plus offers and requests: *I have **some** milk. Would you like **some** tea? Can I have **some** water?*
- **any** — negatives and questions: *I don't have **any** money. Do you have **any** time?*

**Why it matters**: Czech speakers often drop these (*Mám mléko* → *I have milk*). English usually needs *some* or *any* with plural or uncountable nouns. Missing them sounds like a beginner; using the wrong one (e.g. *I have any milk*) sounds broken.

**Contrast examples**:
- I bought **some** bread. (positive)
- I didn't buy **any** bread. (negative)
- Did you buy **any** bread? (genuine question)
- Would you like **some** bread? (offer — expecting yes)

sections.title: much / many / a lot of
sections.content:
**The big three for large amounts.** This is where countable vs uncountable matters most.

- **much** — uncountable, mostly negatives and questions: *I don't have **much** time. How **much** sugar?*
- **many** — countable plurals, positives or negatives: ***Many** people agree. There aren't **many** seats.*
- **a lot of / lots of** — works with **both**; the natural choice in positive statements: *I have **a lot of** friends. There's **a lot of** traffic.*

**Why it matters**: *much* in positive statements sounds formal or unnatural (*I have much time* — wrong). Use **a lot of** instead. Real conversations and exam writing both turn on this.

**Czech-speaker trap**: *I have much friends* — double error (much + countable). Fix → *I have **a lot of** friends.*

diagram.title: much / many / a lot of
rows: not much time, how much money?
many books, not many people, how many?
a lot of friends, lots of water

sections.title: (a) few / (a) little
sections.content:
**The small-amount pair** — and the attitude shift that comes with the article.

- **a few** + countable = **some** (positive feeling): *I have **a few** friends here.* (enough — happy)
- **few** + countable = almost none (negative feeling): *He has **few** friends.* (sad — lonely)
- **a little** + uncountable = **some** (positive): *I need **a little** help.* (a small amount — OK)
- **little** + uncountable = almost none (negative): *There's **little** hope.* (almost no hope — bleak)

**Why it matters**: One small word (*a*) flips the meaning from positive to negative. Czech speakers often miss this — *I have few time* is both grammatically wrong (countable/uncountable) and conveys hopelessness when you just meant 'a small amount'.

**Quick test**: If you mean *some, enough* → use **a**. If you mean *not enough, almost none* → drop **a**.

sections.title: all / most / no / none
sections.content:
**The degree scale** — from 100% down to 0%.

- **all** = 100% (the whole amount/group): ***All** students passed. **All** the water is gone.*
- **most** = the majority: ***Most** people agree. **Most** of the time it works.*
- **some** = an unspecified part (see card 2)
- **no** + noun = zero: *I have **no** time. There are **no** seats.*
- **none** (no noun after, or *none of*): ***None** of them came. How many left? **None**.*

**Why it matters**: *no* and *none* mean the same thing but work differently in the sentence. *No* needs a noun after it; *none* stands alone or takes *of the*. Mix them up and the grammar breaks.

**Czech-speaker trap**: *None students came* — wrong. → ***No** students came* OR ***None of the** students came.*

sections.title: both / either / neither (exactly two)
sections.content:
**For exactly TWO things** — a separate small system. Use these when the group is two, not three or more.

- **both** = the two together: ***Both** answers are correct. I like **both** of them.*
- **either** = one or the other: *You can sit on **either** side. Take **either** one.*
- **neither** = not one and not the other: ***Neither** answer is right. I like **neither** of them.*

**Why it matters**: With three or more, switch to *all / any / none*. Using *both* for three things is a giveaway error.

**Verb agreement note**: *Both* takes plural (*Both are correct*); *neither* + singular noun takes singular (*Neither answer **is** right*).

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors + roadmap
sections.content:
**High-frequency mistakes** to watch for:

- *much friends* → ***a lot of** friends* (countable, positive)
- *many water* → ***much / a lot of** water* (uncountable)
- *I have few time* → *I have **a little** time* (uncountable + positive)
- *I have any money* → *I have **some** money* (positive)
- *I have **money*** (no quantifier) → often needs ***some** money* in English
- *None students came* → ***No** students came* / ***None of the** students came*
- *less people* → ***fewer** people* (countable → fewer; uncountable → less)

**Why it matters**: These appear in every speaking exam, every email, every conversation about quantities. Fixing them gives immediate visible progress.

**Direct practice in this curriculum** (start here after the cards):
- Guided MC (some/any, much/many, few/little)
- Gap fill (mixed quantifiers in context)
- Error correction (the typical traps)

**Next steps**: For the noun side of this — when a noun is *countable* vs *uncountable* in the first place — see [[topic:countable_uncountable|Countable & Uncountable Nouns]]. For the other half of determiners, see [[topic:articles|Articles (a/an/the)]].

questions.question: 'I have _____ milk.' (positive) Which fits?
'How _____ books did you read?' Which fits?
'She has _____ friends.' (a small number, positive) Which fits?
'There isn't _____ time.' Which fits?
'I need _____ help.' (a small amount, positive) Which fits?
I have _____ milk in the fridge.
Do you have _____ sugar?
How _____ people were at the party?
She has _____ friends in London.
There is _____ water left. (almost none)
We have _____ time to finish. (some)
_____ the students passed the exam.
I don't have _____ money.
There are _____ apples on the table. (a small number)
He has _____ experience in teaching.
questions.explanation: Positive statement, uncountable → a lot of.

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have any milk.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How much books did you read?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How many books did you read?
How many books did you read

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has few friends.' (meaning she has some friends) Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'I need a little eggs.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'There isn't some bread.' Correct the sentence.
Wrong: 'How many water do you need?' Correct the sentence.
answers: How much water do you need?
How much water do you need

questions.question: Wrong: 'Less and less people are using public transport.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Fewer and fewer people are using public transport.
Fewer and fewer people are using public transport
questions.explanation: People is countable → use **fewer**, not **less**. **Less** is for uncountable nouns (less time, less water); **fewer** is for countable nouns (fewer people, fewer books).
